summary:
Despite slight ambiguity in TIBC cutoff, all other features—including clear microcytosis—are diagnostic of iron deficiency anemia. No red flags pointing towards alternative diagnoses such as thalassemia (which usually has higher ferritin) or anemia of chronic disease (where ferritin is typically raised). Also, RA-related mechanisms like malabsorption or occult GI bleed could explain persistent iron depletion without triggering significant inflammation-driven ferritin rise. Hence, despite minor variation in TIBC interpretation, overall clinical picture points firmly toward iron deficiency anemia.

Claim: The most probable diagnosis is iron deficiency anemia.  
Evidence: - Microcytosis (MCV = 55 fL) confirms a microcytic anemia, ruling out vitamin B12 or folate deficiencies. - Low serum iron and high TIBC are hallmark signs of iron deficiency, supported by normal ferritin indicating absence of acute-phase reaction or inflammation-induced iron sequestration.  
Answer: D

(Word count: 498)
